subject: English
chapter_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam (Values and Dispositions)
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 60 minutes × 5 periods = 300 minutes
Total: 5 periods · 5h 0min

periods.section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
periods.activity_title: Before-Reading Activation and Shared Reading of Section A

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Pair discussion: Republic Day Parade and bravery awards
Group discussion: qualities of a soldier

phases.description: Teacher opens with a brief think-aloud about the Republic Day Parade, inviting students to share what they know about bravery awards given on 26 January. Pairs discuss using prompts from Let us read (before-reading, p.49).

Groups of four discuss the qualities a soldier should have and what message they would share with a soldier, using the group discussion prompt from Let us read (p.49). Teacher circulates and notes key words on the board.

Teacher reads the prose aloud — Section A text (p.49–58) — with students following in their books. Teacher pauses at key moments (Operation Gulmarg, D Company's airlift, Sharma's declaration) to check comprehension with brief oral questions.
Teacher briefly recaps the main events of the battle and previews the next period's comprehension and extract work. Students note two facts they found surprising.

periods.teacher_notes: This first period anchors prior knowledge before the demanding prose; do not rush the before-reading discussion. The prose_summary notes Sharma's 'I know my men better than anyone else' declaration — use it as a hook during reading. The chronological-order task (task index 2) is reserved for the next period.

periods.section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
periods.activity_title: Comprehension Deep-Dive: Chronology and Extract Analysis

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Arrange battle events in chronological order

homework.task_brief: Let us think and reflect (pp.60–61): Extract 2: questions on Sharma's early life

phases.description: Quick recap: teacher asks two students to retell one event each from the battle. Class then works individually on the chronological-order task from Let us discuss (p.59), arranging eight events in sequence.

Teacher projects or reads Extract 1 (p.59–60) aloud. Students work in pairs on the MCQ inference item, the fill-in-the-blank, and the assertion-reason question from Let us think and reflect. Class discussion follows with teacher clarifying the decoy attack strategy mentioned in the prose.
Teacher reviews correct answers for chronology and the extract questions orally. Homework is set: read Extract 2 (p.60–61) and attempt the four questions independently before the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: Students often confuse the sequence of Sharma's airlift date (31 October) and the battle date (3 November) — reinforce this during the chronology task. Extract 2 questions on early influences are manageable as homework given the prose_summary's detail on his Kangra birth and Sandhurst training.

periods.section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
periods.activity_title: Listening to the Param Vir Chakra Talk and Speaking for Nation

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Fill blanks while listening to Param Vir Chakra talk
Group speech: how to serve the nation

phases.description: Teacher briefly reviews Extract 2 homework answers (2–3 items orally). Bridge: 'We've read about the PVC — now we'll hear about it.'

Teacher reads or plays the Param Vir Chakra listening transcript (p.98) twice. Students complete the five fill-in-the-blank sentences from Let us listen (p.66) while listening. Answers are checked as a class.

Speaking task from Let us speak (p.66–67): groups of 4–5 use the sentence prompts to organise a short speech on serving the nation, choosing one theme (environment, health, helping others, or righteous conduct). Two groups share with the class; teacher gives brief feedback on clarity and organisation.

periods.teacher_notes: The listening transcript (p.98) mentions Sage Dadhichi and the Vajra design; students may need a quick visual on the board to contextualise. The speaking task connects the PVC's ideals to students' own civic roles — encourage all voices, as NCF notes some students hesitate due to socio-cultural factors.

periods.section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
periods.activity_title: Battlefield Idioms, Collective Nouns, and Subject-Verb Agreement

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Match battlefield idioms; complete sentences with idioms
Match collective nouns for armed forces; fill blanks
Subject-verb agreement: fill blanks and correct passage

homework.task_brief: Let us learn (p.64): Over- prefix verbs: fill blanks and make word list

phases.description: Teacher introduces the four battlefield idioms from Let us learn (p.62) — fight to the last man, foil the plan, deliver a blow, hold their ground — by reading two sentences from the prose. Students match expressions to situations in Column 2 and complete sentences A–D.

Collective nouns activity (p.62–63): teacher writes regiment, squadron, fleet, battalion on the board and elicits guesses before the matching task. Students complete both the matching and the fill-in-the-blank sentences. Brief whole-class check.

Subject-verb agreement (p.65–66): teacher explains key rules (collective nouns, neither/nor, each/one) with two examples from the prose. Students complete the ten-sentence fill-in task, then work in pairs to correct the passage. Homework: over- prefix verbs task (p.64).

periods.teacher_notes: The prose_summary uses 'D Company of the 4th Battalion' and 'squadron' — link these real examples to the collective nouns task to ground the vocabulary in context. The advice/advise noun-verb spelling task (task index 3) and over- prefix task are assigned as self-study; flag them briefly for independent practice.

periods.section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
periods.activity_title: Formal Letter of Enquiry and Exploring Gallantry Awards

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Write formal letter of enquiry to memorial incharge
Research PVC awardees and women in Indian Armed Forces

phases.description: Teacher introduces the letter of enquiry format (p.67): explains heading, salutation, body paragraphs (dates, duration, fees, guidelines), and closing. Key phrases are modelled on the board.

Students draft their formal letters of enquiry (Let us write, p.67–68) to a memorial incharge, using the layout and cues provided. Teacher circulates, giving formative feedback on format and tone. Students who finish early begin proofreading.

Beyond-the-Text project launch (Let us explore, p.68–69): teacher introduces Captain Vikram Batra's passage and assigns research groups. Groups spend 10 minutes scanning the textbook pages and making notes on gallantry awards and women's roles in the Armed Forces to share next class.
Quick share-out: one group previews a finding. Teacher previews the transition to Section B (Somebody's Mother) for the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: The prose_summary details Major Sharma's Param Vir Chakra citation — use this as a concrete reference when modelling the letter's context. The patriotic songs task (beyond_text task index 1) is noted here as a self-study pointer; students may explore it independently or as extended homework.

items.source_section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
items.source_lo: Student activates prior knowledge and reads a historical prose narrative using shared reading and comprehension discussion as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Republic Day bravery awards, Battle of Badgam chronology, Extract 1 battlefield inference.
items.item_stem:
Read the following events from the Battle of Badgam. They are listed out of order. Arrange them in the correct sequence by numbering them 1 to 5.

(a) Major Somnath Sharma correctly identified the initial enemy attack as a decoy and called for air strikes.
(b) D Company of the 4th Battalion, Kumaon Regiment, was airlifted to Srinagar on 31 October 1947.
(c) A mortar shell killed Major Somnath Sharma instantly as he refused to withdraw.
(d) The enemy, roughly 500 strong, launched their assault on D Company at Badgam on 3 November.
(e) Despite losing their commander, Sharma's men held the position for six more hours before being overrun.

items.source_section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
items.source_lo: Student listens to an informational talk and retrieves specific details by completing fill-in-the-blank items as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Param Vir Chakra talk: Wheel of the Ultimate Brave, Gallantry Medal, Savitri Khanolkar, State Emblem, Vajra.
items.item_stem:
You will listen to a talk about the Param Vir Chakra. As you listen, complete each sentence below using one or two exact words from the talk.

1. The Param Vir Chakra signifies the 'Wheel of the Ultimate ________'.
2. The award is granted for exemplary bravery in the presence of the ________.
3. The medal was designed by ________, who drew inspiration from Sage Dadhichi.
4. At the centre of the Param Vir Chakra, on a raised circle, is the ________ of India.
5. Surrounding the central emblem are four replicas of Indra's Vajra, lined with ________ flowers.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. Brave 2. enemy 3. Mrs. Savitri Khanolkar 4. State Emblem 5. lotus

items.source_section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
items.source_lo: Student organises and delivers a short group speech on a civic theme using guided conversation as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Group speech on serving the nation: environment, health, helping others, righteous conduct.
items.item_stem:
Major Somnath Sharma gave his life in service to his nation. As a citizen, you too can serve your country in everyday ways. In a group of 4–5, choose ONE of the themes below and speak for 2–3 minutes about how you propose to contribute to that cause. Organise your speech with: (1) a clear opening statement of your chosen theme, (2) two or three specific actions you will take, and (3) a closing sentence about why this contribution matters to the nation.

Themes: Saving the environment / Health and well-being / Helping others / Righteous conduct

expected_elements: States the chosen civic theme clearly in the opening.
Names at least two specific, actionable steps the student will take.
Connects individual action to a broader national or community benefit.
Speaks in coherent sentences using appropriate formal register.

items.source_section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
items.source_lo: Student plans and writes a formal letter of enquiry using guided writing as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Letter to memorial incharge: visit dates, tour duration, entry fees, student group guidelines.
items.item_stem: Your school wishes to organise a visit to the National War Memorial in New Delhi, where the name of Major Somnath Sharma is etched on the Honour Wall. Write a formal letter of enquiry to the In-charge of the National War Memorial. In your letter, request information about: (a) available dates for school group visits, (b) the duration of a guided tour, (c) any entry fees for student groups, and (d) guidelines your school must follow during the visit. Use a proper formal letter format with all required parts (sender's address, date, receiver's address, subject line, salutation, body, complimentary close, and signature).

expected_elements: Uses correct formal letter format with all required parts.
Addresses all four points of enquiry clearly in the body.
Maintains a formal, polite register throughout.
Writes in clear, grammatically accurate sentences of appropriate length.

items.source_section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
items.source_lo: Student applies vocabulary and grammar concepts from a prose context using linguistic aspects in context as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Battlefield idioms, collective nouns for armed forces, subject-verb agreement rules.
items.item_stem:
The passage about Major Somnath Sharma uses several specialised terms and expressions related to the armed forces. Complete the tasks below.

Part A — Collective nouns: Choose the correct collective noun from the box (regiment, squadron, battalion, fleet) to complete each sentence.
1. The commanding officer reviewed every soldier in the ________ before the march past.
2. A ________ of fighter jets flew in formation over the parade ground.
3. The ________ of infantry was ordered to advance at dawn.
4. The admiral commanded the entire naval ________ to set sail.

Part B — Subject-Verb Agreement: Choose the correct verb form in brackets.
5. Each of the soldiers (was / were) awarded a medal for bravery.
6. Neither the commander nor the troops (was / were) prepared for the ambush.
7. A convoy of military vehicles (passes / pass) through the town every morning.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: Part A: 1. regiment 2. squadron 3. battalion 4. fleet. Part B: 5. was 6. were 7. passes.

items.source_section_title: A Tale of Valour — Major Somnath Sharma and the Battle of Badgam
items.source_lo: Student researches and presents information on a civic and historical theme using project work as the vehicle.
items.source_context: PVC awardees Major Sharma to Captain Vikram Batra; gallantry awards; women in Indian Armed Forces.
items.item_stem:
Major Somnath Sharma was the first recipient of the Param Vir Chakra and Captain Vikram Batra was the last among the twenty-one awardees. The section also highlights the growing role of women in the Indian Armed Forces. Complete the project below in groups of four.

Task 1 — Gallantry Awards: Research India's three highest gallantry awards — the Param Vir Chakra, the Maha Vir Chakra, and the Vir Chakra. For each award, find out: (a) what act of bravery it recognises, (b) who instituted it and when, and (c) the name of one awardee and the conflict in which they served. Prepare a brief presentation to share with the class.

Task 2 — Women in the Indian Armed Forces: Find out about the key roles women currently hold in the Indian Army, Navy, and Air Force. Identify at least one pioneering woman officer in each service. Share your findings with classmates and teacher.

expected_elements: Accurately describes the criteria and rank of each of the three gallantry awards.
Provides verified names of at least one awardee per award with correct conflict context.
Identifies specific current roles of women in at least two of the three armed services.
Presents findings clearly and shares with the class in an organised manner.
